source_name: Constitution of the Russian Federation (1993) — Key Provisions
text: CONSTITUTION OF THE RUSSIAN FEDERATION (1993). Chapter 1 — Fundamentals of the Constitutional System: Article 1: Russia is a democratic federal rule-of-law state with a republican form of government. Article 2: The individual, their rights and freedoms are the supreme value. Recognition, observance, and protection of human rights is the duty of the state. Article 4: The sovereignty of the Russian Federation extends to its entire territory. The Constitution has supreme juridical force and direct effect throughout the territory. Article 10: Separation of powers — state power is exercised on the basis of the separation of legislative, executive, and judicial powers, which are independent. Article 15: The Constitution is the supreme law. Laws and other legal acts must not contravene the Constitution. Generally recognized principles and norms of international law and international treaties of the Russian Federation are a component part of its legal system. If an international treaty establishes rules different from those of domestic law, the rules of the international treaty shall apply. Chapter 2 — Rights and Freedoms: Article 17: Human rights and freedoms are recognized and guaranteed in accordance with generally recognized principles and norms of international law. Article 19: All are equal before the law and the courts. Article 20: Everyone has the right to life. The death penalty may be established by federal law as an exceptional punishment for especially grave crimes. Article 21: The dignity of the person is protected by the state. No one may be subjected to torture, violence, or other cruel or degrading treatment. Article 22: Everyone has the right to freedom and personal inviolability. Article 23: Everyone has the right to inviolability of private life, personal and family secrets, and the protection of honour and good name. Article 29: Everyone is guaranteed freedom of thought and speech. Article 46: Everyone is guaranteed judicial protection of their rights and freedoms. Decisions and actions of state organs may be challenged in courts.

RUSSIAN LEGAL INSTITUTIONS. CONSTITUTIONAL COURT: Exercises judicial control over the constitutionality of federal laws and other normative acts. Can declare laws unconstitutional. Resolves disputes on competence between federal state organs. PROCURACY (PROKURATURA): The Procurator General supervises the observance of the Constitution and laws. The procuracy has a dual function: criminal prosecution and supervision of legality. It is a distinctive feature of the Russian legal system inherited from the Soviet period. ARBITRAZH (COMMERCIAL) COURTS: Handle economic disputes between legal entities and individual entrepreneurs. The Supreme Court of the Russian Federation was merged with the former Supreme Arbitrazh Court in 2014. CIVIL AND CRIMINAL CODES: The Civil Code (1994-2006) governs private law relationships. The Criminal Code (1996) establishes crimes and punishments. The Code of Criminal Procedure governs criminal proceedings and includes protections for the accused.
